questions.question: This planet is the closest to the Sun.
This element, with atomic number 79, has the chemical symbol Au.
This physicist developed the general theory of relativity.
This ancient wonder of the world is the only one still standing.
This document, signed in 1215, limited the power of the English monarch.
This empire, lasting from 27 BC to 476 AD, was centered in modern-day Italy.
This fictional wizard attended Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry.
This 1994 film follows the life journey of a man sitting on a bench telling his story.
This singer released the albums 'Purple Rain' and 'Sign o' the Times'.
This is the longest river in Africa.
This country has the most natural lakes in the world.
This strait separates Europe from Asia at Istanbul.
This author wrote 'Romeo and Juliet' and 'Hamlet'.
This dystopian novel features a character named Winston Smith.
This Colombian author wrote 'One Hundred Years of Solitude'.
This sport uses a shuttlecock.
This country has won the most FIFA World Cup titles.
This boxer was known as 'The Greatest' and changed his name from Cassius Clay.
This Austrian composer wrote 'Eine kleine Nachtmusik' and 'The Magic Flute'.
This band released the album 'Abbey Road' in 1969.
This genre of music originated in New Orleans in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
This Italian dish consists of wide, flat pasta layered with sauce, cheese, and meat.
This spice, derived from the Crocus sativus flower, is the most expensive spice by weight.
This Japanese rice wine is brewed using a fermentation process involving koji mold.
This company created the iPhone.
This programming language, created by Guido van Rossum, is named after a British comedy group.
This protocol, the foundation of data communication on the World Wide Web, stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol.
This 1997 James Cameron film features a doomed ocean liner and a famous 'I'm the king of the world!' scene.
This director is known for films like 'Pulp Fiction', 'Kill Bill', and 'Inglourious Basterds'.
This 1982 sci-fi film, directed by Ridley Scott, is set in a dystopian 2019 Los Angeles and follows a detective hunting replicants.
This president, the 16th, is famous for the Emancipation Proclamation and his top hat.
This president purchased the Louisiana Territory from France in 1803, doubling the size of the nation.
This president served the shortest term in office, dying of pneumonia just 31 days after inauguration.
This is the largest land animal on Earth.
This marine mammal is the largest animal to have ever lived on Earth.
This tiny bird, native to Cuba, is the smallest bird species in the world at just over 2 inches long.
This animated show follows a yellow-skinned family in the town of Springfield.
This HBO fantasy series, based on George R.R. Martin's novels, features the Iron Throne.
This British sitcom, set during World War II, follows the misadventures of Captain Mainwaring's Home Guard platoon.
This is the smallest continent by land area.
This African country is home to Mount Kilimanjaro, the tallest peak on the continent.
This landlocked Central Asian country is the largest landlocked country in the world by area.
This force keeps us on the ground and causes objects to fall.
This organ in the human body is responsible for filtering blood and producing urine.
This subatomic particle, theorized by Peter Higgs, was confirmed by CERN in 2012 and is associated with giving particles mass.
This 'King of Pop' performed the moonwalk and released the best-selling album of all time.
This rock band, fronted by Freddie Mercury, is known for 'Bohemian Rhapsody' and 'We Will Rock You'.
This composer went almost completely deaf yet continued to compose, including his famous 9th Symphony.
This Greek god is the ruler of the sea and carries a trident.
This Norse god wields the hammer Mjolnir and is the god of thunder.
In Greek myth, this king was condemned to stand in a pool of water beneath fruit trees, with both receding whenever he reached for them.
This sport is played on a diamond-shaped field with bases.
This tennis player holds the record for the most Grand Slam men's singles titles.
This country has won the most Olympic gold medals in the Summer Games all-time.
This wall, built to keep invaders out of China, stretches over 13,000 miles.
This 1945 conference divided post-WWII Germany among the Allied powers and was attended by Truman, Stalin, and Churchill.
This ancient trade network connected China to the Mediterranean, facilitating the exchange of silk, spices, and ideas.
This is the tallest type of tree in the world, found in California.
This natural phenomenon, caused by charged particles from the Sun hitting Earth's atmosphere, creates colorful lights in polar skies.
This deep-ocean trench in the western Pacific is the deepest known point on Earth's surface, reaching nearly 36,000 feet.
This mustachioed plumber, created by Nintendo, rescues Princess Peach from Bowser.
This sandbox game, created by Markus 'Notch' Persson, lets players mine and craft in a blocky, procedurally generated world.
This 2015 action RPG, the third installment in a Polish studio's fantasy series, features a monster hunter named Geralt of Rivia.
This children's author wrote 'The Cat in the Hat' and 'Green Eggs and Ham'.
This novel by F. Scott Fitzgerald is set in the Jazz Age and features a mysterious millionaire on Long Island.
This Russian author wrote 'War and Peace' and 'Anna Karenina'.
This planet in our solar system is known for its iconic rings.
This astronaut was the first human to walk on the Moon in 1969.
This dwarf planet, discovered in 2005 and larger than Pluto, is the most massive known dwarf planet in the solar system.
This popular breakfast food, often served in a stack with syrup, is made from batter cooked on a griddle.
This fermented Korean side dish is made primarily from salted and seasoned napa cabbage.
This French culinary term refers to preparing all ingredients and having them measured and ready before cooking begins.
This Leonardo da Vinci painting depicts a woman with a famously enigmatic smile.
This Dutch painter, who cut off part of his own ear, created 'The Starry Night'.
This art movement, pioneered by Braque and Picasso around 1907, depicted subjects from multiple viewpoints using geometric forms.
This language is the most spoken native language in the world by number of speakers.
This ancient writing system used by the Egyptians features symbols representing sounds and concepts.
This constructed language, created by L.L. Zamenhof in 1887, was designed to be a universal second language.
This 1994 animated Disney film features a lion cub named Simba.
This director created 'Jaws', 'Schindler's List', and 'Jurassic Park'.
This 1957 courtroom drama, directed by Sidney Lumet, takes place almost entirely in a single jury room.
This social media platform, known for short videos, was originally called Musical.ly.
This author created the fictional universe containing hobbits, elves, and the One Ring.
This Japanese filmmaker directed 'Spirited Away', which won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature in 2003.
This mathematical constant, approximately 3.14159, represents the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter.
This number system, used in computing, uses only the digits 0 and 1.
This sequence of numbers, where each term is the sum of the two preceding ones, begins 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13...
This is the largest state in the United States by area.
This river, the longest in the United States, flows from Minnesota to the Gulf of Mexico.
This U.S. state capital, the highest in elevation at over 5,000 feet, shares its name with a fictional character from 'The Simpsons'.
This 1993 Steven Spielberg film features a billionaire's dinosaur-filled theme park that descends into chaos.
This actress played the lead role of Katniss Everdeen in 'The Hunger Games' film series.
This 1968 Stanley Kubrick sci-fi epic features the AI computer HAL 9000 and a mission to Jupiter.
This British singer-songwriter, known for her powerful voice, released the album '21' and the hit 'Rolling in the Deep'.
This rapper, born Shawn Carter, is one of the best-selling music artists of all time and is married to Beyoncé.
This jazz trumpeter and bandleader recorded the landmark album 'Kind of Blue' in 1959 and is celebrated for pioneering both cool jazz and modal jazz.
This black-and-white bear, native to China, eats almost exclusively bamboo.
This large spotted cat of sub-Saharan Africa is the fastest land animal on Earth, capable of reaching speeds over 70 mph.
This venomous, egg-laying Australian mammal has a duck-like bill, a beaver-like tail, and can detect electric fields with its bill.
This element makes up about 21% of Earth's atmosphere and is essential for the respiration of most living organisms.
This gas, released by burning fossil fuels and absorbed by plants during photosynthesis, is the primary greenhouse gas driving climate change.
This massive particle accelerator, operated by CERN beneath the border of France and Switzerland, was used to confirm the existence of the Higgs boson.
This team sport, played on an ice rink, involves players using sticks to shoot a puck into the opposing team's goal.
This Philadelphia 76ers guard, nicknamed 'The Answer', was famous for his devastating crossover dribble and won the NBA MVP award in 2001.
This British Formula 1 driver has won a record seven World Drivers' Championship titles.
This soft Italian cheese, available fresh or aged, is the classic topping for a Margherita pizza.
This creamy Mexican dip, made primarily from mashed avocado, is commonly served with tortilla chips.
This Italian cured meat, made from salt-cured and seasoned pork belly, is a key ingredient in pasta dishes like carbonara.
This general and statesman, the first president of the United States, led the Continental Army to victory in the American Revolutionary War.
This 1969 NASA mission, crewed by Neil Armstrong, Buzz Aldrin, and Michael Collins, landed the first humans on the Moon.
This French military leader was exiled to the island of Elba in 1814 but escaped to rule France again before his final defeat at Waterloo.
This long-running medical drama, set at a hospital in Seattle, follows the career of Dr. Meredith Grey.
This Netflix sci-fi horror series is set in a small Indiana town and begins with the mysterious disappearance of a boy named Will Byers.
This critically acclaimed AMC drama follows high school chemistry teacher Walter White as he turns to manufacturing and selling methamphetamine.
This city on the Seine River is the capital of France and home to the Eiffel Tower.
This South American country has the continent's largest population and contains the majority of the Amazon rainforest.
This Southeast Asian archipelago nation is the world's largest by number of islands, with over 17,000 spread across the equator.
This singer, known as 'Queen Bey', released the groundbreaking visual album 'Lemonade' in 2016 and is one of the best-selling artists of all time.
This British actress starred as Hermione Granger in the Harry Potter film series and has also served as a UN Women Goodwill Ambassador.
This manga and anime series, created by Masashi Kishimoto, follows a determined young ninja who dreams of becoming the leader of his village.
This Charles Dickens novel, set during the French Revolution, opens with the famous line 'It was the best of times, it was the worst of times.'
This J.D. Salinger novel follows the teenage narrator Holden Caulfield as he wanders New York City after being expelled from prep school.
This 19th-century Russian author explored morality and psychology in novels such as 'Crime and Punishment' and 'The Brothers Karamazov'.
This company, founded by Larry Page and Sergey Brin in 1998, operates the world's most widely used internet search engine.
This encrypted messaging app, used by over two billion people, was acquired by Facebook in 2014 for approximately $19 billion.
This open-source operating system kernel, created by Linus Torvalds in 1991, powers everything from Android phones to most of the world's web servers.
This Italian Renaissance artist painted the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel and sculpted the famous marble statue of David.
This Spanish surrealist painter, known for his flamboyant personality and waxed mustache, created 'The Persistence of Memory' featuring melting clocks.
This Norwegian artist depicted a figure with an agonized expression against a swirling orange sky in his famous 1893 painting.
This white, winged horse of Greek mythology sprang from the blood of Medusa after she was slain by Perseus.
This Athenian hero sailed to Crete and killed the half-man, half-bull creature living in King Minos's labyrinth.
This Norse shapeshifter and trickster, born of two giants but adopted into Asgard, is the god of mischief and father of monsters like Fenrir and the Midgard Serpent.
This president, who served four terms from 1933 to 1945, led the nation through the Great Depression and most of World War II.
This 5th president issued an 1823 doctrine warning European nations against further colonization or interference in the Americas.
This 27th president is the only person in American history to have served as both U.S. President and Chief Justice of the Supreme Court.
This Nintendo action-adventure franchise features a green-clad hero named Link on a quest to rescue Princess Zelda.
This massively popular battle royale game, released by Epic Games in 2017, is known for its shrinking storm, cartoonish style, and building mechanics.
This 2013 PlayStation exclusive follows a hardened smuggler named Joel as he escorts a teenage girl named Ellie across a post-pandemic America.
This is the name of the star at the center of our solar system and the closest star to Earth.
This NASA spacecraft, launched in 1977, is the farthest human-made object from Earth and became the first to enter interstellar space.
This powerful astronomical event occurs when a massive star exhausts its fuel and collapses, triggering a catastrophic explosion that can briefly outshine an entire galaxy.
This boot-shaped European country is home to Rome, Venice, and Milan, and is surrounded by the Mediterranean Sea on three sides.
This country is the largest in the world by land area, spanning 11 time zones across Eastern Europe and northern Asia.
This South American capital city sits on the Pacific coast and is home to over ten million people, making it one of the most populous cities in the Americas.
This pop icon, known as the 'Material Girl', dominated the 1980s with albums like 'Like a Virgin' and 'True Blue'.
This Compton-born rapper's 2012 album 'good kid, m.A.A.d city' is widely regarded as one of the greatest rap albums ever made.
This Irish rock band, fronted by the singer known as Bono, released the anthemic albums 'The Joshua Tree' and 'Achtung Baby'.
This annual championship game, played at the end of the NFL season, is the most-watched television event in the United States.
This Milwaukee Bucks forward, nicknamed 'The Greek Freak', won back-to-back NBA MVP awards in 2019 and 2020.
This American golfer, nicknamed 'The Golden Bear', holds the all-time record for major championship wins with 18 titles.
This colorful arc of light appears in the sky when sunlight passes through water droplets in the air, typically after rain.
This giant cactus, native to the Sonoran Desert of Arizona and Mexico, can grow up to 40 feet tall and live for more than 150 years.
This scientific theory, introduced by Charles Darwin in 1859, explains how species change over successive generations through natural selection.
This 1977 George Lucas space opera, which opens with the crawl 'A long time ago in a galaxy far, far away...', launched one of the most successful film franchises in history.
This 1999 sci-fi film starring Keanu Reeves depicts a dystopian world in which humans are unknowingly living inside a computer simulation.
This 1974 Francis Ford Coppola sequel interweaves the story of Michael Corleone's rise to power with flashbacks to his father Vito's early life as an immigrant.
This element makes up about 78% of Earth's atmosphere and is essential to the production of proteins and DNA in living organisms.
This type of blood vessel carries oxygenated blood away from the heart and distributes it throughout the body's tissues.
This principle in quantum mechanics, formulated by a German physicist in 1927, states that the more precisely you know a particle's position, the less precisely you can know its momentum.
This four-year conflict, fought between 1861 and 1865, pitted the Union states of the North against the Confederate states of the South.
This German dictator rose to power in 1933, led the Nazi Party, and was responsible for the Holocaust and starting World War II.
This 1789 event, in which Parisian citizens stormed a royal prison fortress, is widely regarded as the symbolic beginning of the French Revolution.
This Harper Lee novel, set in the American South, follows lawyer Atticus Finch as he defends a Black man falsely accused of a crime.
This British author created the fictional detective who solves mysteries from Baker Street alongside his partner Dr. Watson.
This ancient Greek epic poem, attributed to Homer, chronicles the events of the Trojan War, centering on the wrath of the hero Achilles.
This hot brewed beverage, made from roasted and ground beans, is one of the most widely consumed drinks in the world.
This classic Italian dish is made by slowly adding warm broth to rice while stirring, resulting in a creamy, starchy texture.
This cold Spanish soup, popular in summer, is blended from raw tomatoes, cucumber, peppers, garlic, olive oil, and vinegar.
This streaming service, which began as a DVD-by-mail rental company, is now the world's largest subscription video-on-demand platform.
This actress played the beloved character Rachel Green on the long-running sitcom 'Friends'.
This Canadian rapper, who first gained fame as an actor on the teen drama 'Degrassi', became one of the best-selling music artists of all time.
This long-running singing competition, hosted for many seasons by Ryan Seacrest, launched the careers of Kelly Clarkson and Carrie Underwood.
This beloved NBC sitcom, set in a New York City apartment building, features the characters Jerry, George, Elaine, and Kramer.
This critically acclaimed HBO crime drama, set primarily in Baltimore, is considered one of the greatest TV series ever made and examines the drug trade, police work, schools, and the media.
This eight-legged arachnid spins silk webs to capture insects for food.
This large African bird is both the tallest and heaviest bird on Earth and also holds the record as the fastest running bird.
This highly intelligent sea creature has three hearts, blue blood, eight arms, and can instantly change the color and texture of its skin.
This is the number of sides on a hexagon.
This mathematical concept, represented by the symbol ∞, describes a quantity larger than any finite number.
This type of number cannot be expressed as a simple fraction and has a decimal expansion that never terminates or repeats, such as √2 or π.
This American pop artist is famous for his silk-screen prints of Campbell's Soup cans and colorful portraits of Marilyn Monroe and Elvis Presley.
This American abstract expressionist, known for his 'drip painting' technique, created large canvases by pouring and splattering paint across them.
This 15th-century Flemish painter, a pioneer of oil painting technique, is known for the Ghent Altarpiece and his extraordinary attention to detail.
This narrow strait connects the Atlantic Ocean to the Mediterranean Sea and separates southern Spain from northern Morocco.
This mountain range forms the natural border between France and Spain, separating the Iberian Peninsula from the rest of Europe.
This Southeast Asian archipelago nation, composed of over 7,600 islands, is the only predominantly Christian country in Asia.
In tennis, this word describes a score of zero for a player.
This American swimmer won 8 gold medals at the 2008 Beijing Olympics, setting the record for most gold medals won by any athlete at a single Olympic Games.
This prestigious annual road cycling race, held primarily in France each July, is the most famous event in professional cycling.
This social media platform, founded by Mark Zuckerberg at Harvard in 2004, became the world's largest social network.
This wireless networking technology allows devices to connect to the internet over short distances without a physical cable.
This computing model refers to storing, managing, and processing data on remote internet-connected servers rather than on a local machine.
This Greek demigod, son of Zeus and a mortal woman, was tasked with performing twelve seemingly impossible labors as punishment.
This creature of Greek mythology, born of Queen Pasiphae and a sacred bull, had the head of a bull and the body of a man and lived in the labyrinth of Crete.
This multi-headed dog of Greek mythology stands guard at the entrance to the underworld, preventing the dead from leaving and the living from entering.
This country music legend, known for songs like 'Jolene' and 'I Will Always Love You', also owns the Dollywood theme park in Tennessee.
This Seattle grunge band, fronted by Kurt Cobain, released the landmark album 'Nevermind' in 1991, bringing alternative rock into the mainstream.
This jazz vocalist, nicknamed 'Lady Day', is considered one of the greatest jazz singers of all time and recorded the haunting protest song 'Strange Fruit' in 1939.
This 2016 augmented reality mobile game sent millions of people outdoors to catch virtual creatures using their smartphones.
This 2011 open-world RPG from Bethesda, set in a snowy Nordic province filled with dragons, features the iconic battle shout 'FUS RO DAH'.
This 2017 Nintendo open-world game featuring the hero Link was praised for revolutionizing the action-adventure genre with its physics-based puzzles and climbing mechanics.

questions.display: The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild

questions.question: This president was in office when the September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks occurred and subsequently launched the War on Terror.
This 35th president, the youngest ever elected to the office, was assassinated in Dallas, Texas in November 1963.
This 26th president, known as 'The Rough Rider', established the U.S. national park system and was the youngest person ever to assume the presidency.
This form of precipitation consists of ice crystals that form unique six-sided structures as they fall from clouds.
This sudden release of energy in the Earth's crust causes the ground to shake and can trigger tsunamis, landslides, and widespread destruction.
This ecological process describes how a community of plants and animals gradually develops and changes over time in a habitat, eventually reaching a stable state called a climax community.
This is the name of the spiral galaxy that contains our solar system.
This planet, the fourth from the Sun, is called the Red Planet due to iron oxide on its surface and is the focus of many future crewed mission plans.
This icy celestial body, composed of dust and frozen gases, develops a bright glowing tail when it passes close to the Sun.
This punctuation mark, placed at the end of a sentence, signals that the writer is asking a direct question.
This ancient language of the Roman Empire gave rise to Spanish, French, Italian, Portuguese, and Romanian, and is still used in Catholic Church ceremonies.
This literary device assigns human characteristics to non-human subjects or abstract concepts, as in the phrase 'the wind whispered through the trees'.
This 2010 Christopher Nolan thriller follows a team of specialists who enter people's dreams to plant an idea in a target's subconscious.
This 2003 Peter Jackson film, the third in his epic fantasy trilogy, won the Academy Award for Best Picture along with 10 other Oscars.

valid: the lord of the rings the return of the king
lord of the rings return of the king

questions.display: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King

questions.question: This 1927 Fritz Lang silent sci-fi film depicts a dystopian future city divided between an elite ruling class living above and an oppressed working class laboring below.
